speaker: interviewer
text: The date is November 17th, 1995. The survivor is Freda Weiss, and this is tape two. Mrs. Weiss, please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the-- the Hungarian took us, very rough, by the hands, and threw us on the trains, in those cattle trains. In fact, when they threw my mother, she fell on the steps from the train. And she start screaming. Because her ribs wasn't healed yet, from the fall before. And we went in, in the trains. There was thousands of peoples, childrens, men, women, old, young, babies, crying in the arms of their mothers. My sister, Sarah, had the baby, [? Rachel. ?] She didn't have what to give him to eat. She had the baby feed-- breastfeeding. And she didn't have nothing in the breast. And the baby slept and cried, and cried. And she-- we took out the-- from the mouth, the wetness from the mouth. And we put it in the baby's mouth. Everybody, like, my mother, myself. And Chanale laughed. Because she thought it's a joke. She was three years old. And we put it in the baby's mouth, then the baby should have some wet in the mouth. [SIGHS] People was making on the train. We didn't have where to go. It-- the-- the-- the wagons was full with dirt. Yeah. A lot-- some people died. Some people fell down and died. And everybody stepped on top of them. And just people couldn't cope with such a misery, what it was, there. This is unbelievable. But we didn't have no way. It was a little, very small little window which we looked through the window. And we saw well, the train was running like crazy, very, very-- day and night. Day and night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we talked. We said, where are they taking us? You know, one said, they're taking to Poland, to work. They going to select who is-- who is for work, and who is not for work. So what they going to do with the babies? So nobody had an answer. We just couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that's how it was for five days. After five days, this train stopped. We didn't know if it-- and we saw 'Judenrein, Auschwitz.' 'Arbeit macht das leben suess,' in German. This means, work makes life sweet. It was a big sign. When we arrived on the train we thought, maybe they put us to work. 'Arbeit macht das leben suess' means that they going to put us to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were relaxed. We were. When they opened the doors from the wagons with-- with such a madness. They said, [GERMAN]. This means out, out, out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one on top of the other, was hundreds of people crowded, very much. One on top of the other. Everybody want to run. They shouldn't be. Because they took the stick and they beat everybody who didn't-- who didn't went in a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: So everybody went out on the trains-- [? train, ?] [? and out. ?] And the SS, a lot of women SS and men SS, they put us in the line, five in the line near the train. And we saw so much clothes on the side of the street. So much [NON-ENGLISH], clothes. How you call it, [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we couldn't imagine why. And then, I-- then we know. The SS, when they put us five in the row, they came to each one and took away everything. Even the few things what we had, in the hand. Nothing, they left us, nothing. Just what was that was on-- on our body. It was a lot-- and then everybody start [? marschieren. ?] And the SS, at every second foot was an SS, or a woman or a man. And in-- in about half a mile, there was the Gestapo. And the Gestapo and the SS, it was about a hundred of them with sticks in their hands. And everybody has to [? marschier ?] in the front of them. And they picked. And when they saw my mother with Chanale by the hand, they said, [GERMAN]. You go to the left! My sister with the baby in the hand, you go to the left! Hollering. When I came without babies and alone, they looked at me. And they said, [GERMAN]. You go to the right. I was started screaming. I want to go with my mother! I like my mother! I want to go with my mother and sister, what. And they started beating me with a stick over my back and my head. You go to the right. They needed me for work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the right. My mother walked with the sister with the kids, and a lot of thousands of people. My mother looked in the back, if-- if I come.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hinted with my hand, bye. Bye, ma. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. And that was the end, that I saw my mother, my sisters. After that, they put, again, us in five in the row, after the selection. And the SS women went with us to the barracks where they had the showers. Passing to the-- go into the showers there, through the barrack. We saw the crematorium. We saw the flames. And I start screaming. I see the skelett of my mother. Look at the flame. That is the skelett of my sister. I imagine it cannot be. But I imagined. I saw the flames. They took us in, in those shower house. And one in the left side was the shower room. And the right side was the gassing room, where they gassed the people. [? Acrossed, ?] like we walked with music.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a full band with Mozart music, with-- with Steinbach, with all kinds of music, playing music very loud. Why did it? Because the people by the-- in the gas chambers screamed, hollered for a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: They climbed the walls. You could hear. Because when I was in the shower room, I could hear climbing the walls, scratching the walls. They want to run. They-- they want to escape. But it was the gas room. They gassed them there. And then they-- they have a under-- where they had-- where the bodies when it was gassed went out-- [LAUGHS] Went out. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had the showers. They came and they-- they cut our hair. Whole hair, and they shaved us all over. We was naked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the SS and Mengele-- then was Mengele-- came in. And other Gestapo men with their boots, and walking and looking. And every directions. And [? skipped ?] and said, you dirty Jew. You the-- and we were naked. And they looked at us and spit on us. They spit. And-- and we--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't recognize each other. I had my girlfriends from my hometown. I have people from my hometown. We looked-- who? We didn't recognize any-- because it-- it was-- it was terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Would have been better if they would have killed us at home, in our home, from where they took us, than to go through all that, what we went through. And that was a few hours like that. And we hear the music. And we hear this-- the-- the gas. The people hollered. Not long. But they brought in-- when one party, when thousands went out after the gassing they brought in, already, other thousands. Other thousands. So we always heard screaming.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to mine friend, also cousin, we were together in one lane. I  said, [? Suri ?] did you hear screaming? You hear scratching? She says, yes, I hear. What could it be? We still didn't want to believe!

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there was kapos from Pole-- Poland. They was there, already, three, four years before us. And they came to us and said, you dummies, you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: What are you asking for? Don't you see the flames? What do you think is that? They burning wood? They're burning your fathers and mothers and children. Those told us. I said, you are crazy. What you talking about? She says, you gonna find out later. I'm here already, long time enough. And I know what's going on. What was the date that you arrived in Auschwitz? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The day, it-- it was April, around April, 15, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [LAUGHS] --we couldn't do nothing. We just had to go naked in front of the SS people. And they spitted on everybody. And they laughed. They made circus of us. And they laughed. And we had to shut up. We had to just walk. And when it was already, about a few thousand gone over, all the selection, they took us in a barrack in-- in-- in Auschwitz. And I was in barrack A, number 14. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was-- and we came in there. It was no wood floor, just-- just the earth. And there was three rows of little-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] little house-- not houses, but little like-- where they put us, to stay, one on top of the other. One then-- then the second. Then the third.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like bunks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bunks.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bunks, yeah. And we were 12 people in one bunk. The bunk was big as the table.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we was crammed in, one on top of the other. And we hit each other. We screamed at each other, oh, you're killing me. You're killing me. But she said, what can I do? Where shall I go? What shall I do? Overnight we find a few bodies, dead. They suffocated in the bunks.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first night, I had to go. I had to go. And I begged a [NON-ENGLISH]-- this means a Polish girl who was there. And she was the commander-- listen, I have to go. Otherwise I'll do it here. She says, I'm going to ask the SS woman. She came back. She says, OK, go ahead. But quick! Quick! Quick! I went out to go to that hole. They had just a hole outside, where everybody just stood and-- and not far from that hole was the crematorium. And I saw, again, the flames. And the flames, in the middle of the flames, I saw a leaf with black words on it. And I recognize that Jewish words. So I-- I knew it's a leaf from a [? sedar, ?] from a book, a Jewish book.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't go, anymore, to the-- where I had to go. But I went with my palm and-- up and to catch the leaf. The leaf came straight to my palm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Really, it's unbelievable. When I say it, I feel like my face is-- the leaf come in my palm. And I read it, [HEBREW]. I read it. And it melted in-- in-- in ash. And it blew away.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought to myself, something mean-- this means something, that God will help me. The Shema Yisrael came before-- before they kill me? Because we was sure they gonna kill us. So I went back. I didn't go. I-- I didn't need to go anymore. I didn't. I went back to that barrack. And I told that-- those people, the women. I couldn't tell that the SS should hear.

speaker: interviewee
text: If they would hear me saying that, they would take me right away and put me in crematorium. Because wasn't allowed to say the truth. And I told the women, listen.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a little leaf, a Jewish book, [NON-ENGLISH]. And it said, Shema Yisrael. And I said the Shema Yisrael. They said, oh you crazy. How-- what you talking about? I went back to my place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we was there in the morning, 4:00 in the morning. They called. They blew with the horns. They blew-- they waked us up, 4:00 in the morning. Everybody had to go down from the-- from that barrack. Out, out out. And Mengele and more kapos. And they put us, and five in a row. About a thousand-- a few thousand women. And they came to count us. And they count if we are all there. And there was mothers with their children, 15-years, girls. And their mothers was very scared, that they're going to see such a young-- children-- girls, that they're gonna pick them, put them in crematorium.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I used to [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what it mean, [NON-ENGLISH]? Mine-- mine face, I don't want to-- shall I show it with-- like that, that my-- mine face should become red, healthy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was very pale and very scared. And whoever wasn't nice and healthy and red in the face, they picked from the row. And they took them away. You never saw them again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the mothers used to [NON-ENGLISH] their children. Let me make you nice colors in the face. Because they're going to take you away from me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't want to live. I'm going with you. If they take you, I'm going with you. [LAUGHS] Many, many was taken from the mothers. And never saw them again. And many, many took the mothers. And the children didn't see the mothers. There was young mothers. 30, 32, 28 years. No, they were all dead. If to have 15 year, they were 30-- because in Europe, they used to get married by 15. My mother was very young, married, with my father. And this was-- then after Mengele was and the kapos, and they made, already, the selection, they picked a lot of girls. And they eliminate them. The [? SSes, ?] they brought the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: The food, a soup. What kind of soup? It was from grass, nothing with nothing. A empty soup. They said, you go sit down on the ground, everybody. If we made a row. A row, you know? And the middle was empty.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to sing. Later on, it wasn't at first. Later on I used to sing for the women in that-- I used to sing [YIDDISH]. I used to sing with a melody. I-- I lost my voice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean, what you were singing?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, Mama, take me to you. Mama, darling, where are you now? Mama darling, you've forgotten me? That I am your only child? In Yiddish, it rhymes. That's the better expression, it rhymes very nicely. So-- And then the [? SSes, ?] they knew that I sing. And Yiddish is similar, like German, language. A lot of the words are the same. The [? SSes ?] looked and looked and looked until they find me. And they say, [GERMAN] Freda! [GERMAN]. You, Freda, you go to sing in the middle, outside. It could have been seven, eight o'clock in the morning. Four o'clock, they took us out. A few hours we stood, everybody. And then it could be around eight, nine o'clock. We didn't have watches. We didn't have nothing. And I start singing with my feet under me. And I was sitting down in the middle. And I was singing, [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what this means? Outside it's very cold. The wind is blowing. Mine eyes are very sad, that what people are doing with us [NON-ENGLISH], Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also it's rhyming, and it's a whole song. It's longer. But I don't want to do it. And they loved it. They gave me a-- one more portion soup for that, the [? SSes. ?] And if they want to give me more I said, no. No, I don't want you giving me more. Then the other people will not have nothing. You can't do that. I have enough. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And after being outside like that, for a few hours, march inside. They didn't say go, march inside. We went back in those little-- how you call it? Bunks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bunks. Yeah. And this was every day. Every day, the same. At night time out, and counting. And in the morning. They wanted to see if somebody runs away. The-- the woman whom they picked out from the row and they put them to death, they had marked down, they knew. So they had-- they knew how many people has to be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day were there selections made?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day was Mengele, there. Every day he was going around with the sticks, [? drawn, ?] like that. Like that. And-- and beating. And-- and-- and-- [SIGHS] Oy, God. And after five weeks living like that, in Auschwitz, not-- very little food. You know, they put in the-- in the soups medication, that we should lose the menstruation. We-- and a lot of people had very much cramps. A lot of girls, young girls they had pain and cramps. But they wasn't allowed to tell. If they would tell they have cramps, they don't feel good. They pick them out, and go. Go to death. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day-- hmm-- I was very sick. I had a [NON-ENGLISH] on my breast. A-- something very big, full with pus. And it hurt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had 104 fever. I didn't know that it's 104. That doctor-- the book. I have the book. You want to take the book? I'll show you. We'll look at it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Perl, Gisella. She is born in Sighet, where Elie Wiesel is born. In fact, they had a relationship. And her name was Perl, Gisella. She was the Auschwitz doctor. And she was in our barrack. And I went to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I cried to her, how can I go out? I'm so sick. I'm falling apart. Look.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says-- and she took the-- she took the-- the fever, how much I have. Oh, she says, you are very sick. You cannot go out. But if I don't go out, they will kill the other people. Whoever was missing, the other people was responsible for each one.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say that she was in your barrack, what do you mean by that? That she was-- she was-- she took care of your barrack? She was living in your barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was living in our barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Living in your barrack. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had one room-- room, if the girls got sick, to examine them and to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the Nazis know that she was doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She-- The Nazis know. She was picked as the doctor, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And-- and then she says, you know what? You go when-- going to be in the morning say appel. Say appel means the counting. You go under the bed. I'm going to cover you with a lot of blankets. We take blankets from the other girls, and we cover you. And they're not going to see. They're going to see blankets only. I said, OK. That's fine with me, if you think it's OK. I went to sleep. I mean, to sleep-- we went in at night, in the-- in the-- in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: --bunks. In the bunks. Mine father, [NON-ENGLISH], died at home. He didn't go to concentration camp. He died a natural death. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in the middle of the night, I fall asleep. And I dreamed-- dreamt my-- my father comes and tells me, [NON-ENGLISH]. Dear child, don't worry. You're going to be OK in the morning. You're going to be healed. He said to me, you're going to be fine. I got up in the morning. My blister, big thing, exploded. It came out, the pus. I didn't have no fever. And I went in to the doctor. It was close to the barrack. And I showed her. She says, oh, thank God. Thank God. So go out to say appel, to the counting. I did. I went out. And I was OK. She-- she put something to-- against infections, and things like that. She was very good, that Perl, Gisella, she was some woman. Very good. She was the doctor in Hadassah, in Jerusalem, after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you and the other women in your barrack put to work at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not in-- no?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Some barracks, not ours, had work. They went to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the clothes, where it was so much clothing and teeth, and baby's clothes, and-- and-- Tallis and Tefillin, from the men, for religious people, everything was in the-- how you call that, [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewer
text: In piles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Piles. So there was barracks with women who went to pick-- separate the shoes, separate the dresses, separate the teeth, separate the hair. They pick the hair, what they cut off from the people, they-- they use-- they had use of it. They sold them. They made brushes. They made different kind of things. So that was barracks who worked. That was the arbeitslager. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And but our barrack didn't work. That's why they pick-- after six weeks they came in the barrack. And they picked whoever they want for work. They picked out and shipped us to another city.
